coredata.dc:title: Unraveling the phosphorus-nitrogen bridge in carbon quantum dots/carbon nitride for efficient photodegradation of organic contaminants

coredata.dc:description: Limited transport and migration of photoinduced charges in polymeric carbon nitride (g-C3N4) within the planar layers remain a great challenge for achieving favorable photocatalysis. Herein, phosphorus-doped carbon quantum dots (PCDots) implanted intra-layer carbon nitride nanosheet (PCDots-C3N4) photocatalyst with phosphorus-nitrogen (P–N) bridging was prepared. The 13C and 31P of nuclear magnetic resonance, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and density functional theory calculations confirmed the formation of a P–N bridge. Additionally, implanting PCDots enlarged the π-π conjugated electronic structure and modulated the plane structure of g-C3N4. The atomic-level connection by the P–N bridge dramatically extended the delocalization effect and facilitated the intramolecular charge transfer. PCDots-C3N4 demonstrated efficient photodegradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) and bisphenol A (BPA) than both g-C3N4 and CDots-C3N4, reaching 76% and 100%, respectively. The possible degradation pathways of TC and BPA were also discussed. This work provides an efficient strategy for rapid electron transfer to form a new paradigm for wastewater remediation.

head.ce:title: Unraveling the phosphorus-nitrogen bridge in carbon quantum dots/carbon nitride for efficient photodegradation of organic contaminants

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Limited transport and migration of photoinduced charges in polymeric carbon nitride (g-C3N4) within the planar layers remain a great challenge for achieving favorable photocatalysis. Herein, phosphorus-doped carbon quantum dots (PCDots) implanted intra-layer carbon nitride nanosheet (PCDots-C3N4) photocatalyst with phosphorus-nitrogen (P–N) bridging was prepared. The 13C and 31P of nuclear magnetic resonance, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and density functional theory calculations confirmed the formation of a P–N bridge. Additionally, implanting PCDots enlarged the π-π conjugated electronic structure and modulated the plane structure of g-C3N4. The atomic-level connection by the P–N bridge dramatically extended the delocalization effect and facilitated the intramolecular charge transfer. PCDots-C3N4 demonstrated efficient photodegradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) and bisphenol A (BPA) than both g-C3N4 and CDots-C3N4, reaching 76% and 100%, respectively. The possible degradation pathways of TC and BPA were also discussed. This work provides an efficient strategy for rapid electron transfer to form a new paradigm for wastewater remediation.

The phosphorus-doped carbon quantum dots (PCDots) implanted intra-layer carbon nitride nanosheets (PCDots-C3N4) photocatalyst with phosphorus-nitrogen (P–N) bridging is prepared via facile copolymerization approach. The atomic-level connection by P–N bridge dramatically extended the delocalization effect and facilitate the intramolecular charge transfer for efficient photocatalytic aqueous pollutants by PCDots-C3N4. The degradation effects of tetracycline hydrochloride and bisphenol A were 76% and 100%, respectively.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor-based photocatalysis provides as a sustainable avenue to address environmental pollution because of its high activity, low cost, sustainable driving force (solar), and no secondary contamination [1,2]. Among the investigated photocatalysts, polymeric carbon nitride (g-C3N4), as a metal-free polymer candidate, has attracted tremendous interest on account of its sustainability, facile accessibility, beneficial band gap (approximately 2.7 eV), and high physiochemical stability [3–6]. However, bulk g-C3N4 exhibits a low photocatalytic performance because of its inherent shortcomings such as its the high photocarrier recombination rate and poor conductivity [7].

Many strategies have been developed to address the limitations of stacked g-C3N4. Among them, the fusion of small π-π conjugated monomers such as carbon quantum dots (CDots) into the framework of g-C3N4 was proposed to be effective to promote the separation of photocarriers [8–10]. CDots anchored in the existing framework of g-C3N4 can improve the π-π conjugate system among the adjacent heptazine rings and produce micro-regional heterostructures to induce an intrinsic driving force for the separation of photogenerated charges [11,12].

Despite the improved performance induced by CDots, fewer efforts have been devoted to the control of the atomic-level connection between CDots and g-C3N4. Additionally, the noncovalent modification cannot obviously change the electron distribution of CDots modified g-C3N4 due to the ultra large π region [13,14]. To achieve more effective and faster charge transfer, it is critical to create covalent bonds to establish electronic transmission channels between CDots and g-C3N4. In addition, some heteroatoms such as boron (B) [15,16], nitrogen (N) [17,18], and phosphorus (P) [19,20] were doped in CDots to improve their electronic properties and surface chemical reactivity. Compared with B or N, P was difficult to dope in the CDot because of its larger atomic radius than carbon. Additionally, P exhibits n-type semiconducting properties and donor substitutional defects, which can form in carbon clusters, thereby increasing surface reactivity and catalytic properties [21]. Recently, the formation the phosphorus-nitrogen (P–N) polar covalent bonds in g-C3N4 has received much attention because it offers a highway for interlayer charge transport [22,23]. Therefore, it is expected that incorporating CDots within the interlayers of g-C3N4 through a unique P–N bridge would accelerate the charge separation and transfer.

Herein, to explore the effect of the P–N bridge on charge separation and transfer, phosphorus-doped carbon quantum dots (PCDots) implanted carbon nitride (PCDots-C3N4) via the P–N bridge is designed by the co-polymerization of urea and PCDots. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and density functional theory (DFT) calculation are used to confirm the formation of P–N bridges. Tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) and bisphenol A (BPA) are selected as model pollutants to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of PCDots-C3N4. Furthermore, the possible degradation pathway of TC and BPA by PCDots-C3N4 are also discussed. This work will provide an efficient route to facilitating charge transfer within polymer semiconductors via constructing planar homogeneous hybrids, providing a green and sustainable approach to environmental remediation.
Urea (6 g) was placed into a crucible with a cover and calcined at 550 °C for 3 h at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 in a muffle furnace. Finally, the sample was naturally cooled and obtained as g-C3N4.

An electrochemical method was used to prepare CDots [9]. To begin, two graphite rods positioned at 3.5 cm were used as the cathode and anode. Then, NaOH (0.4 g) was added to 100 mL of electrolyte containing deionized (DI) water and ethanol (1:99). A 30 V static constant voltage was passed between the two electrodes until the liquid changed from clear to dark brown. This electrolyte was then filtered using a 220 nm filter and was subsequently dialyzed for three days with a 3500 Da dialysis bag. Finally, the remaining solution was collected and freeze-dried into powder, and the powder was named CDots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of phosphorus doping carbon dots (PCDots)
ce:section.ce:para: PCDots were synthesized using the same method [24]. A graphite rod and a platinum electrode were used as the working electrode and counter electrode, respectively. To supply a phosphorus source, sodium phytate (0.1 mol L−1) was used as the electrolyte. A voltage of 5 V was scanned for 12 h. The liquid was then filtered using a 220 nm filter and dialyzed with a 3500 Da dialysis bag for three days to remove the electrolyte. Finally, the solution was ground after freeze drying to powder and designated as PCDots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots implanted carbon nitride nanosheets (CDots-C3N4)
ce:section.ce:para: To begin, urea (6 g) and of CDots (5 mg) were mixed well. Then, the mixture was added to a crucible and calcined in a muffle furnace at 550 °C for 3 h at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. Finally, the materials were obtained as CDots-C3N4 after cooling to room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of phosphorus doped carbon dots implanted carbon nitride nanosheets (PCDots-C3N4)
ce:section.ce:para: PCDots-C3N4 was synthesized by the thermal polymerization of urea and PCDots. First, urea (6 g) and PCDots (X mg, where X = 1, 5, 10, 20, or 40) were mixed. Then, the mixture was transferred into a crucible with a cover and calcined at 550 °C for 3 h at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 in a muffle furnace. After cooling to room temperature, the product with 5 mg PCDots was named PCDots-C3N4, and the others were designated as PCDots–C3N4–X (X = 1, 10, 20, and 40).

In a typical run for tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) photodegradation, 25 mg of the catalyst was added to 50 mL of TC solution (10 mg L−1), and then the suspension was stirred for 40 min in the dark to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium. A 300 W xenon lamp with a cut-off filter (420 nm) was employed as the visible light source. After the xenon lamp was switched on, 3 mL of the suspension was sampled at fixed time intervals (1, 3, 7, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 min) and filtered by a 0.45 μm microporous filter to remove the solid photocatalyst. The concentration of TC was measured using an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer at the detection wavelength of 357 nm. For the stability test, the catalysts were collected by filtration (0.45 μm microporous filter) after each cycle in the four cycle experiments. The experiments of bisphenol A (BPA) photodegradation were identical to those of TC except for the concentration analysis, and more details are provided in the Supporting Information.

PCDots-C3N4 was synthesized by the one-step thermal polymerization of urea and PCDots (Fig. 1 a). The morphologies of the catalysts were obtained by the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The PCDots sample presented a nearly spherical structure with a regular size and uniform distribution (Fig. S1a), and the lattice spacing was observed as d = 0.201 nm (Fig. S1b), which corresponds to the (101) crystal plane of graphite [11]. It can be observed from Fig. S1c that the particle size of PCDots was approximately 1.5–4.0 nm. In addition, CDots had a spherical structure (Fig. S1d) and the same lattice spacing (Fig. S1e) as that of PCDots. In contrast, the particle size distribution of CDots is between 0.25 and 3.0 nm (Fig. S1f) which is smaller than that of PCDots, indicating that the diameter of CDots increased after P doping.

Unlike pure g-C3N4 (Figs. S2a and S2d) and PCDots–C3N4–40 (Figs. S2c and S2f), many pores (marked by yellow circles) were produced in CDots-C3N4 (Figs. S2b and S2e) and PCDots-C3N4 (Fig. 1b and c). The pores were formed because the decomposition of CDots and PCDots were restrained by the protective effects of CO2 and NH3 in the process of copolymerization of the urea [11]. During the copolymerization, PCDots connected with urea-derived g-C3N4 through the P–N bridge, resulting in the formation of many holes by the removal of H2O molecules (Fig. S3). However, the addition of excessive PCDots may block the holes, such as PCDots–C3N4–40 which has a dense bulk structure (Figs. S2c and S2f). PCDots could not be observed in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of PCDots-C3N4 (Fig. 1d) because of the small additional amount of PCDots compared to g-C3N4 [9,25]. However, the thermogravimetric curve showed that approximately 27% of PCDots remained after heating under the same condition used to synthesize PCDots-C3N4 (Fig. S4). In addition, in the STEM element mapping image of PCDots-C3N4 in Fig. 1e, PCDots-C3N4 contained four elements: C, N, O, and P. P was evenly distributed, confirming that PCDots were successfully implanted into g-C3N4. Moreover, the thickness of PCDots-C3N4 was 2.5 nm (Fig. 1f), corresponding to 7–8 layers [26]. The π-π conjugated structure of PCDots with a more quantum confinement effect may improve the electron transport ability in the in-plane direction [27].

The crystal structure of the samples was analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD). Fig. S5a showed the XRD results of CDots and PCDots. The wide peak (18–28°) centered at 21° demonstrated that CDots had a highly graphitized internal structure, which is in good agreement with the HRTEM results [28]. The broad peak of PCDots located between 19° and 38° and the deviation were mostly due to the incorporation of P, which decreased the degree of crystallinity of the derived carbon dots. All of the prepared catalysts had two diffraction peaks at 13.1° and 27.3° (Fig. 2 a), corresponding to (100) in-plane packing motifs and the stacking of the (002) interlayer of g-C3N4, respectively [29]. Since a small amount of CDots and PCDots were doped on g-C3N4 and due to the low surface decoration, the diffraction patterns of CDots and PCDots were difficult to observe in PCDots-C3N4 and CDots-C3N4 [30].

The chemical structures of CDots and PCDots were studied based on the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Fig. S5b). The absorption peaks at 3420 cm−1 of both samples were attributed to –OH. PCDots had a P–C bond peak at 1450 cm−1, showing that P was successfully doped onto PCDots. The absorption in the region from 2850 to 2924 cm−1 was related to C–H groups. Moreover, the peaks at 1610 and 1198 cm−1 were identified as CC and CO, respectively [24]. The FT-IR spectra of the samples (Fig. 2b) showed similar characteristic peaks. Specifically, the broad peak in the range of 3000–3600 cm−1 represented the functional groups of –OH and –NH2 [31]. The tensile vibration mode of the aromatic C–N heterocycle was mainly shown at 1200–1600 cm−1 [32], and the characteristic peak at 812 cm−1 was attributed to the triazine unit (C3N3) [33]. The FT-IR spectra of PCDots-C3N4 and CDots-C3N4 were similar to that of pure g-C3N4 due to the minor doped amount of PCDots and CDots as well as the generally identical peak positions for PCDots, CDots, and g-C3N4. Nonetheless, these XRD and FT-IR results demonstrated that the graphitized structure of g-C3N4 could be well preserved after compositing [34,35].

To investigate the effect of implanted PCDots and CDots on the pore structures, nitrogen adsorption and desorption experiments were performed. All of the prepared catalysts displayed type IV isotherms with H3 hysteresis loops, implying the existence of mesoporous structures [36]. The pore volume and pore size were further determined by the pore structure distribution curves in Fig. 2d. The specific surface area (SSA) of g-C3N4, CDots-C3N4, PCDots- C3N4, and PCDots–C3N4–40 were 43.43, 94.83, 81.58, and 47.40 m2 g−1, respectively (more parameters are displayed in Table S1). PCDots-C3N4 and CDots-C3N4 had more mesoporous and microporous structures than g-C3N4. The increased porous structures can be ascribed to the disintegration of CDots and PCDots which was restricted by the protection of CO2 and NH3 during the urea copolymerization process. The porous structure can effectively increase SSA and expose more surface active sites, which can absorb more pollutants on the catalyst. Thereafter, the absorbed pollutants are degraded by the catalyst. The pore size and pore volume of PCDots–C3N4–40 were similar to those of g-C3N4, showing that the excessive doping of PCDots was not conducive to increasing the SSA. This phenomenon is supported by the TEM results.

To further explore the P coordination environment in PCDots, XPS analysis was performed on CDots and PCDots (Fig. S6). P was doped onto CDots through the formation of P–C, P–O, and PO, bonds, and more details are discussed in the Supporting Information. The surface elemental compositions and valence distribution of PCDots and g-C3N4 were also measured by XPS. As shown in Fig. S7a, all of the prepared catalysts contained the elements C, N, and O. Owing to the ultralow amount of PCDots implanted into PCDots-C3N4, it was difficult to identify P in the full survey, but the P 2p spectrum appeared in Fig. 3 b. As shown in Fig. 3a, the doping of CDots and PCDots changed the C skeleton structure (Fig. 3a). The strong peak at 288.3 eV was assigned to sp2 N–CN, and the another peak located at 284.6 eV was ascribed to sp2-hybridized aromatic bonds [37,38]. It is worth noting that the peak at 284.6 eV of PCDots-C3N4 and CDots-C3N4 was stronger than that of g-C3N4, suggesting again that PCDots and CDots have been introduced into the g-C3N4. Fig. S7b depicts the N1s spectra, where three characteristic peaks appeared at 398.8, 399.9, and 400.3 eV, corresponding to C–NC, N–(C)3, and C–N–H, respectively [39]. In the P 2p spectrum (Fig. 3b), the peaks centered at 133.5 and 132.5 eV were derived from P–N and P–C, respectively [40,41]. In addition, as shown in Fig. 3c, the same characteristic signals displayed at 157.8 and 166.2 ppm for all of the catalysts represented the two positions of C (1) and C (2), respectively, in the melon unit [42,43]. This result directly proves that the catalysts were composed of heptazine structures, similar to the XRD results. The presence of phosphorus in PCDots-C3N4 is supported by Fig. 3b and d. P solid-state NMR (Fig. 3d) of PCDots-C3N4 showed the three signal peaks located at 1.5, −9.1, and −20.1 ppm which were ascribed to PO, P–C, and P–N, respectively [41,44–46]. The above analyses suggest that PCDots were implanted into g-C3N4 via the P–N bond. The appearance of the PO and P–C bonds suggests that P-linked carbocycle ring was introduced into g-C3N4.

The absorption ability of visible light changed dramatically when PCDots and CDots were embedded in the samples. As shown in Fig. 4 a and b, the PCDots and CDots implanted photocatalysts exhibited a slight redshift at the absorption edge compared with g-C3N4. The redshift was due to the quantum confinement effects of PCDots-C3N4 and CDots-C3N4 [11,47]. The larger porous structures and better optical absorption of CDots-C3N4 than PCDots-C3N4 may be ascribed to the greater fusion of CDots into the framework of g-C3N4. During the copolymerization process, the existence of P may promote the decomposition of CDots, resulting in fewer doped PCDots. Therefore, a higher amount of CDots may be incorporated into g-C3N4, leading to the increased porous structures and improved optical absorption of CDots-C3N4 than PCDots-C3N4. Although the CDots-C3N4 have more pores and higher optical absorption than PCDots-C3N4, the formation of the P–N bridge in PCDots-C3N4 can facilitate the separation of charge carriers and increase the photocatalytic performance. The absorption of light in the range of 420–800 nm was enhanced upon the decoration of PCDots and CDots on lamellar g-C3N4, which would facilitate the production of photocarriers. The band gaps of the photocatalysts were calculated by the Kubelka-Munk equation (1) and Tauc equation (2):

Accordingly, the band gaps of g-C3N4, CDots-C3N4, and PCDots-C3N4 to be 2.80, 2.63, and 2.74 eV, respectively (more details in the Supporting Information). (Fig. 4b). The XPS-valence band (XPS-VB) (Fig. 4c) was used to determine the valence band (VB) edge potential of the prepared catalyst. The VB values of g-C3N4 CDots-C3N4, and PCDots-C3N4 were 1.99, 2.16, and 2.09 V, respectively. Thus, the band gap structures of the photocatalysts were derived in Fig. 4d. Clearly, the VB edges of CDots-C3N4 and PCDots-C3N4 moved to the positive direction compared with g-C3N4, which was conducive to achieving efficient optical absorption of visible light to enhance photocatalysis.

Photoluminescence (PL), low-temperature solid-state paramagnetic resonance (EPR), electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS), and photocurrent tests were conducted to determine the generation, separation, and transfer of photoinduced electrons and holes [48,49]. As shown in Fig. 5 a, all of the prepared catalysts exhibited the same peaks at approximately 470 nm, while the peak intensity of PCDots-C3N4 declined sharply, indicating that the PCDots-C3N4 catalyst had a low recombination rate of the charge carriers. Contrary to the tendency of PL, the peaks of PCDots-C3N4, CDots-C3N4 and g-C3N4 decreased sequentially in the solid EPR tests (Fig. 5b), suggesting that PCDots-C3N4 contained more unpaired electrons, which is beneficial for photo-redox reactions. In addition, the tensor parameters (g) of the three materials were all 2.003, indicating that there were unpaired electrons in the conjugated clusters of the carbon nitride aromatic rings [50,51]. As shown in Fig. 5c, PCDots-C3N4 showed the smallest arc radius among all catalysts, indicating that PCDots-C3N4 had the lowest charge transfer resistance. In addition, the photocurrent tests were conducted on the catalyst under visible light irradiation (Fig. 5d). PCDots-C3N4 had the higher photocurrent intensity than g-C3N4 and CDots-C3N4, indicating that the inner layer implanted PCDots helped to reduce the recombination of electrons and holes. The improved charge separation and inhibited recombination can be ascribed to the bent in-plane structure of PCDots-C3N4, which accelerated charge transport within the layer.

To explore the oxidation performance of the derived photocatalyst, the degradation efficiencies of TC and bisphenol A (BPA) were measured. As shown in Fig. 6 a, PCDots-C3N4 exhibited the highest degradation efficiency toward TC, and the degradation rate reached approximately 76% at 10 min under visible light illumination, about four times higher than that of g-C3N4 (18%). The high-efficiency degradation performance of PCDots-C3N4 is because PCDots can extend the π-π conjugated structure of g-C3N4, increase the delocalization effect, and facilitate the intramolecular charge transfer induced by the polarization of the P–N bridge [52]. In addition, the first-order-rate constant (k) of PCDots-C3N4 (k = 0.141) for TC degradation was relatively high, approximately 7.1 times that of g-C3N4 (k = 0.0199) (Fig. 6b). The efficiency was higher than or compatible with the reported g-C3N4 or composite photocatalysts displayed in Table S2. Additionally, after four cycles of TC degradation by PCDots-C3N4, the degradation efficiency experienced only a slight decrease (Fig. 6c), indicating that PCDots-C3N4 had excellent stability. BPA was also completely degraded by PCDots-C3N4 within 40 min under visible irradiations (Fig. 6d).

The liquid spin-trapping experiment was conducted on ESR to detect the free radical species in the PCDots-C3N4 photodegradation system. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as •OH and O2 •− were captured by 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) in water (Fig. 7a and S8a) and methanol (Fig. 7b and S8b) under the simulated visible light. In the dark, no signals for •OH and O2 •− could be observed. Moreover, the signals of ·OH and ·O2ˉ strengthened under visible light, suggesting that •OH and O2 •− can be produced in the TC and BPA photocatalytic process, respectively.

To shed further light on the mechanism of the photocatalytic reaction, selective ROS trapping experiments were conducted. Isopropanol (IPA) and triethanolamine (TEOA) were selected as the trapping agents for hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and holes (h+), respectively [53]. Superoxide radicals (O2 •−) can be scavenged by aerating N2 to eliminate the dissolved oxygen. As shown in Fig. 7 c, IPA had no inhibitory effect on TC degradation with PCDots-C3N4. Nevertheless, the degradation efficiency of TC decreased to 22% (60 min) and 57% (60 min) by PCDots-C3N4 after N2 filling and the addition of TEOA, respectively. Therefore, it can be concluded that O2 •− and h+ were the most important contributors while •OH have minor contribution to TC removal. For the degradation of BPA, after the inhibition of O2 •−, h+ and •OH, the degradation efficiencies were 19%, 27%, and 55%, respectively (Fig. S9). This means that O2 •−, h+, and •OH also promoted the BPA photodegradation process, and O2 •− had the greatest contribution. The reason why O2 •− plays an important role in the TC and BPA degradation process is that they all have electron-donating groups such as amino (−NH2), hydroxyl (−OH), and methyl (−CH3) groups, which tend to react with O2 by electron transfer reactions [54].

Based on the photocatalytic performance evaluation and the free radical capturing experiment, a possible photocatalytic mechanism can be drawn under the irradiation of visible light. The schematic diagram of the photocatalytic mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 7d. Under the irradiation of visible light, the electrons in VB of PCDots-C3N4 will cross the forbidden band and transfer to the conduction band (CB), while the holes remain in the valence band. As modified by PCDots, the electrons easily migrated to PCDots via the P–N bridge. As the CB position of PCDots-C3N4 (−0.65 V) was more negative than the standard redox potential of O2/O2 •− (−0.33 V) [55], the oxygen in the air and water combined with the electrons to form superoxide radicals (O2 •−) to conduct photodegradation. In addition, the holes left in VB had a strong oxidizing ability and can directly degrade pollutants. Since the VB potential of PCDots-C3N4 (2.08 V) was more positive than the standard redox potential of OH−/•OH (1.99 V) [56], some residual h+ can combine with the water molecules adsorbed on the surface of the catalyst to form hydroxyl radicals (•OH), which had only a minor contribution to TC oxidation (Fig. 7c). Simultaneously, the holes in the VB had a strong oxidizing ability, which directly degrades pollutants.

Furthermore, high performance liquid chromatography and mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) instrumentation were used to identify the produced intermediates during the photodegradation process of TC and BPA with PCDots-C3N4, respectively. The mass to charge ratio (m/z) of deprotonated TC was 445. After 60 min of degradation, many signals were observed by HPLC-MS at the low m/z positions (m/z = 290, 256, 275, and 114) in Fig. 8 . Thus, TC was transferred into intermediate products with a low molecular weight. Ultimately, the CC and C–C bonds of intermediate products will be further attacked by ROS and h+, leading to ring opening and further decomposition into mineralized products such as CO2 and H2O. Therefore, according to the above analysis, the possible degradation pathway is illustrated in Fig. 8e. Based on the HPLC-MS results, some intermediates and possible degradation routes of BPA are shown in Figs. S10 and S11, respectively. The main procedure of BPA degradation is hydroxylation. First, the monohydroxylated-BPA (A) was formed by electrophilic attack of ROS such as O2 •− and •OH [57]. The monohydroxylated-BPA was then degraded further to produce 4-isopropanolphenol (D). In addition, the BPA was cleaved to produce compound (B) and compound (C) by β-scission and hydroxylation reactions, respectively [58,59]. Furthermore, compound (C) was oxidized by ROS to compound (F). By ring opening, all of the intermediates were decomposed to simple organics such as compound (E), which were finally degraded to CO2 and H2O.

CDots and PCDots with different configurations as graphene clusters were embedded in a 3 × 3 plane g-C3N4 matrix. Fig. 9 shows the computational results. After geometry optimization, the conjugated system converged quickly and yielded evenly distributed and physically feasible structures. In contrast to the smooth sheets of g-C3N4 (Fig. 9a) and CDots-C3N4 (Fig. 9b), PCDots-C3N4 was significantly deformed, and the basal plane was twisted (Fig. 9c and S12). The calculated band gap (BG) in the g-C3N4 and PCDots-C3N4 were 1.37 and 0.28 eV, respectively, while CDots-C3N4 showed zero BG. This is qualitatively consistent with the experimental results that followed the order of BG (g-C3N4) > BG (PCDots-C3N4) > BG (CDots-C3N4). The smaller BG results from the DFT calculation could be attributed to the use of generalized gradient approximation (GGA), which would underestimate the BG value [60]. It can be noted that, using a larger CDots-C3N4 and PCDots-C3N4 unit lattice that contains one CDots or PCDot but more triazine C3N4 elements will result in an increased BG approaching g-C3N4 in a large unit cell. The BG reduction, as indicated in both the experimental and DFT results, helps to enhance light absorption, which results in a redshift to the absorption edge, increasing the generation of electron-hole pairs which will participate in photocatalytic reactions. Further, when the BG becomes narrower, the electron orbitals will come closer to, even overlap with, the Fermi surface, which will significantly reduce the interfacial Schottky barrier [33] and facilitate the hot-carrier transfer between g-C3N4 and the quantum dots, ultimately benefiting the surface redox reactions [11].

The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of g-C3N4, CDots-C3N4 and PCDots-C3N4 were also compared. Both HOMO and LUMO were homogeneously distributed over the entire plane of the triazine mesh in pristine g-C3N4 (Fig. 9d and g). In contrast, the orbitals are concentrated around the isolated quantum domains within CDots-C3N4 (Fig. 9e and h) and PCDots-C3N4 (Fig. 9f and i). This prevents the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes, thus elongating the carrier lifetime and promoting catalytic effect [61].

In summary, PCDots-C3N4 composites were successfully synthesized through a one-step thermal method using urea and PCDots as the precursors. Moreover, PCDots were implanted into g-C3N4 by the P–N bridge through XPS and NMR analysis. P–N bridges were able to accelerate the charge transfer and optimize the energy band structures. DFT results showed that the enlarged π-π conjugated and bent plate structure of PCDots-C3N4 promoted the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes within the layer and expanded the light absorption range. Therefore, the photodegradation rates of TC and BPA by PCDots-C3N4 reached 76% and 100%, respectively. The degradation pathway of TC was revealed via product analysis on HPLC-MS. This work proposes a channel for fast electron transfer in photo-responsive polymers for clean environmental purification.
